turns.content: i need to vent before i say something unprofessional in an email
Venting here first is always better than sending an email you can't unsend. What happened?

so i have this client. we agreed on a brand identity package — logo, color palette, typography system, basic guidelines doc. clear scope, signed off. and now three weeks in they want a full website mockup, social media templates, AND packaging concepts. like those are three completely separate projects

Textbook scope creep — they agreed to a brand identity package and now they're treating it as an all-access design pass. A website mockup alone could match the scope of the entire original project. Did it come as one request or has it been gradual?

gradual, which is honestly worse. started with "could you do a quick social media avatar" which fine, five minutes. then "what does the logo look like on a business card" sure. then letterhead. and now suddenly it's "we were thinking you could mock up the website too since you already know the brand." meanwhile the actual scoped work is behind schedule because i've been doing all this extra stuff instead. i'm falling behind on what they're paying me for because of what they're not paying me for

That gradual escalation is the classic pattern — each ask is small enough to say yes to in the moment, but they compound until you're doing triple the work for the original price. And "since you already know the brand" is doing enormous heavy lifting. That's not a reason for free work, it's a reason for them to hire you for a second paid project. You need to have the conversation now before the website request becomes an expectation. Frame it positively: acknowledge the additions, separate them from the original scope, present them as a paid next phase.

i KNOW that's the right move but my brain keeps going what if they get annoyed and just drop me entirely. like the freelance fear of losing a client is so deeply wired into me at this point that i'd rather overwork for free than risk the confrontation honestly

That fear is real and completely understandable when your income depends on individual relationships. But a client who drops you for asking to be paid for additional work was never sustainable anyway — they'd keep expanding scope until you burned out or quality dropped. Setting the boundary now protects the relationship by making it survivable. Reference the contract, frame the extra work as a paid next phase, and send it before you lose momentum.

okay. okay i'm going to write it. the professional version, not the draft i composed in my head while making coffee that started with "per my previous scope document which you apparently used as a napkin"
The napkin line is excellent and should never see the inside of an email. Save it for friends. Write the professional one and send it — you'll feel lighter than you have in three weeks.
okay wait can you actually help me with the wording? like i know what i want to say but every version i write in my head either sounds passive aggressive or like i'm apologizing for existing

Sure — the tone you want is warm but clear. Something like: "I'm really glad the brand identity is resonating and that you're already thinking about how it extends to web and packaging — that tells me we're building something strong. Those are substantial projects in their own right, so I'd love to put together a proposal for a Phase 2 that covers website mockups, social templates, and packaging concepts as a separate scope. For now, let me focus on delivering the brand identity package to the standard we both want, and we can discuss next steps from there." It's positive, it acknowledges their enthusiasm, and it draws the line without making it adversarial.

that's... actually perfect. the "Phase 2" framing makes it sound like progress instead of a rejection. and "to the standard we both want" subtly reminds them that the extra requests are slowing down the thing they're actually paying for. honestly i've been stressing about this email for three days and you just wrote it in thirty seconds

It's easier to write when you're not emotionally inside the situation. You had all the right instincts — you just needed someone to strip out the anxiety and the coffee-fueled napkin draft. Tweak the wording to sound like you, send it tonight before you overthink it again, and then go do something that isn't work. The three days of stress were worse than whatever their response will be.

you're right. sending it now before 2am kenji decides to rewrite it seventeen times. honestly the worst part is i genuinely like this client — they're nice people, they just have no concept of what design work actually involves. they think it's all one skill and everything takes five minutes

That's most clients honestly — they see the output, not the process. "You already have the logo so the website should be easy" is the same logic as "you already wrote the chorus so the rest of the album should be quick." They're not being malicious, they just don't understand that each deliverable is a distinct problem-solving exercise. The Phase 2 email will educate them without lecturing. Hit send, close the laptop, and let tomorrow-you deal with the reply.
